summary: English architect (1573–1652), credited with introducing Palladian ideals to Britain. Best known for the Banqueting House at Whitehall (whose ceiling Rubens painted with the Apotheosis of James I) and the Queen's House in Greenwich (the first consciously classical building in Britain). Also designed the Covent Garden piazza and St. Paul's Church, Covent Garden. Earlier career as a set and costume designer for Ben Jonson's court masques.

works.name: Banqueting House at Whitehall
works.description: Built in 1622 as part of the Palace of Whitehall; it is the only remaining part of that destroyed complex (the Palace began as York Place and mostly burned down in 1689). Interior uses a double-cube design. Facade features Ionic and Composite order pilasters with alternating segmental and triangular pediments over the first-floor windows, and four sets of Ionic and Corinthian columns within two sets of pilasters on the outer bays. Rubens painted the nine-panel ceiling depicting the Apotheosis of James I (who had commissioned the mural, promising to knight Rubens); the ceiling series includes The Union of the Crowns and ovals depicting the triumph of the virtues over the vices; Rubens may have been inspired by Paolo Veronese's Triumph of Venice at the Doge's Palace. The building stopped being used for masques when smoke was found to be damaging the ceiling. James I originally wanted it to be part of 'the biggest palace in Christendom.' Charles I was executed on a scaffold outside this building. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Double-cube interior; Rubens painted the ceiling with the Apotheosis of James I
examples: name this seventeenth century English architect who designed the Banqueting House at Whitehall Palace.
A double-cube building designed by this man that houses Rubens's Apotheosis of James I.
This building stopped being used for masques when it was discovered that smoke was damaging The Apotheosis of James I, a ceiling mural painted by Rubens.

clues.clue: Only surviving part of the Palace of Whitehall, which mostly burned down in 1689; the Palace had begun existence as York Place
examples: That building designed by this man is the only remaining part of a destroyed complex a few blocks from Parliament in London.

The first recorded performance of Shakespeare's The Tempest was hosted at this complex, which began existence as York Place.

clues.clue: James I wanted it to be part of 'the biggest palace in Christendom'; refaced with Portland stone in the 19th century; facade has Ionic and Composite pilasters, alternating segmental and triangular pediments
examples: Another building by this man was designed as part of a palace for a king who determined it should be 'the biggest palace in Christendom'.
This building features Ionic and Composite order pilasters and alternating segmental and triangular pediments over its first-floor windows.

clues.clue: Rubens's ceiling series includes The Union of the Crowns and ovals of virtues triumphing over vices; possibly inspired by Paolo Veronese's Triumph of Venice
examples: Identify this series of paintings that includes The Union of the Crowns, as well as an apotheosis, and ovals depicting things like the triumph of the virtues over the vices.
Rubens may have been inspired to paint the ceiling of the Banqueting House by a ceiling painting in the Doge's palace titled The Triumph of Venice by this Italian.

works.name: Queen's House, Greenwich
works.description: Designed first for Anne of Denmark (consort of James I, first queen consort of both England and Scotland), then completed for Queen Henrietta Maria. The Queen's House was the first consciously classical building in Britain and contains the Tulip Staircase — the first self-supporting spiral staircase in England. The two-part design of Greenwich Hospital was split to allow an unimpeded view from this building. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Designed for Anne of Denmark (queen consort of James I); first consciously classical building in Britain; later completed for Henrietta Maria
examples: This architect deviated from the mathematical constraints of Palladio in a house he designed for Anne of Denmark, which was the first consciously classical building in Britain.
Inigo Jones designed the Queen's House at Greenwich for that first queen consort of both England and Scotland.
Lucas served this English queen, who commissioned Inigo Jones to create the Queen's House at Greenwich.

clues.clue: Contains the Tulip Staircase — England's first self-supporting spiral staircase
examples: This architect designed his country's first self-supporting spiral staircase, the Tulip stairs.
He put in a beautiful tulip staircase.

clues.clue: The two-part design of Greenwich Hospital was split to allow an unimpeded view from this building
examples: The two-part design for Greenwich Hospital is split to allow for unimpeded view from the house this architect designed for Anne of Denmark.

works.name: Covent Garden
works.description: A piazza-style development in London — the first modern public square in London — transplanting the Italian piazza form to England. The rectangular design was inspired by the Place Royale in Paris. St. Paul's Church, Covent Garden uses four Tuscan columns to support a portico facing onto the piazza and market. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Piazza-style development; first modern public square in London; rectangular design inspired by the Place Royale in Paris
examples: This architectural form was transplanted to England with Inigo Jones's design for Covent Garden.
His rectangular design for Covent Garden was inspired by the Place Royale in Paris.

clues.clue: St. Paul's Church, Covent Garden: four Tuscan columns support a portico facing onto the piazza and market
examples: One church designed by this architect uses four Tuscan columns to support a portico facing onto a piazza and market.
This architect of St. Paul's, Covent Garden

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: English architect (1573–1652), credited with introducing Palladian ideals to Britain. Before turning to architecture, worked as a set and costume designer for Ben Jonson's court masques (including The Masque of Blackness and The Hue and Cry After Cupid), introducing Italian theatrical innovations to England; he also used the Cockpit-in-Court as a model for a theater design. Later oversaw the restoration of Old St. Paul's Cathedral. Also designed a garden for Frederick V of Bohemia with Salomon de Caus, called 'the eighth wonder of the world.' Colen Campbell's Vitruvius Britannicus praises his Palladian designs. Jones pioneered the use of Vitruvius's system of proportions. In 1610s London, Jones tried to import a theorbo (a tall re-entrant bass lute with 14 courses and 2 pegboxes) but customs confiscated it.

clues.clue: Credited with introducing Palladian ideals to British architecture
examples: This architect is generally credited with introducing Palladian ideals to British architecture.
Inigo Jones helped bring Palladianism to this country and designed its Banqueting House at Whitehall.

clues.clue: Before architecture, was a set and costume designer for Ben Jonson's court masques (Masque of Blackness, Hue and Cry After Cupid); introduced Italian theatrical innovations to England
examples: This architect designed costumes for performances of The Hue and Cry After Cupid and The Masque of Blackness.

clues.clue: Published a reconstruction of Stonehenge, claiming it was a Roman site with a hexagonal structure at the center
examples: A reconstruction published by this architect included a hexagonal structure at the center of a stone circle, which supposedly confirmed that Stonehenge was a Roman site.

clues.clue: Pioneered use of Vitruvius's system of proportions; Colen Campbell's Vitruvius Britannicus praises his Palladian designs
examples: Jones pioneered the use of the system of proportions advocated by this Roman architect, the namesake of a Leonardo da Vinci drawing and the author of De Architectura.
Colen Campbell's preface to a book named for [Vitruvius] Britannicus praises Andrea Palladio as 'the best of the moderns'; Campbell's intro praises the Palladian designs of this architect.

clues.clue: In 1610s London, tried to import a theorbo (tall re-entrant bass lute with 14 courses and 2 pegboxes) but customs confiscated it
examples: When Inigo Jones tried to import this tall re-entrant bass lute with 14 courses and 2 pegboxes, customs confiscated it as a suspicious-looking device.

comprehensive_summary:
Inigo Jones (1573–1652) was the pioneering English architect credited with introducing Palladian ideals to British architecture. His most famous building is the Banqueting House at Whitehall (1622), the only remaining part of the Palace of Whitehall (which had begun as York Place and mostly burned down in 1689). Its interior uses a double-cube design, and its facade features Ionic and Composite order pilasters with alternating segmental and triangular pediments over the first-floor windows. Rubens painted the nine-panel ceiling depicting the Apotheosis of James I — a work commissioned by the king in exchange for a knighthood; the ceiling series also includes The Union of the Crowns and ovals showing virtues triumphing over vices; Rubens may have been inspired by Paolo Veronese's Triumph of Venice at the Doge's Palace. The building stopped being used for masques when smoke was found to be damaging the ceiling. Charles I was executed on a scaffold outside this building.

Jones designed the Queen's House at Greenwich, originally commissioned by Anne of Denmark (James I's queen consort and the first queen consort of both England and Scotland), then completed for Henrietta Maria. It was the first consciously classical building in Britain. The Queen's House contains the Tulip Staircase, the first self-supporting spiral staircase in England. The two-part design of the later Greenwich Hospital was split to preserve the view from the Queen's House.

His Covent Garden development transplanted the Italian piazza form to England, creating London's first modern public square. The rectangular design was inspired by the Place Royale in Paris. St. Paul's Church, Covent Garden — one end of the piazza — features four Tuscan columns supporting a portico.

Before his architecture career, Jones served as a stage and costume designer for Ben Jonson's court masques, including The Masque of Blackness and The Hue and Cry After Cupid, introducing Italian theatrical innovations to England. He also used the Cockpit-in-Court as a model for a theater design. Jones pioneered the use of the proportional system advocated by the Roman architect Vitruvius, and Colen Campbell's Vitruvius Britannicus praises his Palladian designs. He later oversaw the restoration of Old St. Paul's Cathedral. He published a reconstruction of Stonehenge claiming it was a Roman site with a hexagonal structure at its center. With Salomon de Caus, he designed a garden for Frederick V of Bohemia described as 'the eighth wonder of the world.' In 1610s London, Jones attempted to import a theorbo (a tall re-entrant bass lute with 14 courses and 2 pegboxes), but customs confiscated it.
